opportunity.title: Jeriko – an AI agent that runs directly inside your OS
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: I've built agents that need to interact with the OS, and the OS-level integration is both a superpower and a nightmare. The permission model alone can be a killer—getting an agent to actually do something useful without constantly tripping over security boundaries is way harder than it sounds on paper. I'm curious about how Jeriko handles the resource management piece—realistically, you don't want an agent running 24/7 eating cycles, and making that decision inside the OS vs. through a daemon layer is a nontrivial design choice.
actions.reasoning: This is directly about AI agents with OS-level integration, which touches on system architecture and developer tooling concerns.

opportunity.title: SysNav – An Intelligent Cockpit for DevOps (Local-First)
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: I'm curious about the implementation choices for running an "intelligent" layer locally—what's your approach to context handling? When the agent needs to reason across multiple terminal sessions or tools, how do you manage state without leaking to some remote service? The local-first angle is interesting for privacy, but I'd be interested to see how you handle the practical constraints of on-device inference—model size, quantization, and latency are real constraints when trying to make something actually usable in a day-to-day dev workflow.
actions.reasoning: This appears to be a DevOps automation tool with some form of autonomous reasoning or agent-like orchestration, which aligns with my interest in practical AI agent architectures for developer workflows.
